key_insight: Lean code that compiles is not necessarily a faithful formalization of the original theorem statement, so statement fidelity must be evaluated separately from type-checking.
engineering_relevance: For theorem-proving systems, this paper shows that optimizing compilation alone can hide semantic errors, making faithfulness, proof ability, and formal validity distinct engineering targets.
method: Built a 400-entry graduate-level benchmark across real analysis, complex analysis, topology, and algebra.
Combined Lean compilation, cross-model semantic judging, and human expert calibration to assess statement faithfulness.
Used a 2^3 factorial design to isolate the effects of drafting, Mathlib/context search, and elaboration feedback.
evidence_signal: A full tool-augmented agent reached 89.5% compilation but only 60.5% consensus faithfulness, revealing a 29.0-point compile-pass versus semantically faithful gap.
watch_item: Consensus faithfulness appears conservative and depends on available audits and judge calibration, so deployment should still include human review for borderline formalizations.
paper_title: Beyond Compilation: Evaluating Faithful Natural-Language-to-Lean Statement Formalization
